title: Keywords introduced in later specifications should be avoided as identifiers
description: While keywords introduced in later standards can legally be used as identifiers in code compiled to earlier standards, doing so will eventually cause problems. Such code will cause compile errors if (when) the compiler is upgraded, and fixing those errors could be difficult and painful. Additionally, such misuse of keywords has the potential to thoroughly confuse people who are unfamiliar with the code base, possibly leading them to introduce additional errors.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
// Using C++11 keywords as identifiers in C++98 code
int auto = 5; // Noncompliant - 'auto' is keyword in C++11
int decltype = 10; // Noncompliant - 'decltype' is keyword in C++11
int nullptr = 0; // Noncompliant - 'nullptr' is keyword in C++11

// Using C++20 keywords as identifiers in C++17 code
int concept = 1; // Noncompliant - 'concept' is keyword in C++20
int requires = 2; // Noncompliant - 'requires' is keyword in C++20
int co_await = 3; // Noncompliant - 'co_await' is keyword in C++20
compliant:
// Using proper identifiers that won't conflict
int automatic = 5; // Compliant
int declaration_type = 10; // Compliant
int null_pointer = 0; // Compliant

// Using descriptive names instead of future keywords
int constraint = 1; // Compliant
int requirement = 2; // Compliant
int awaitable = 3; // Compliant

checks.message: Keywords from later C++ standards should not be used as identifiers
